text:
0.3 seconds to launch.
Faster than the screen catches up to your finger.

Notion: 2.1s. Apple Notes: 0.6s. SimpleMemo: 0.3s.

"Fast" only means anything when it's measured. Method and raw data are public → simplememofast.com/en/blog/fastest-note-app-iphone-2026/

Anyone can call an app fast.
Far fewer can prove it in numbers.
That's the only reason we keep measuring.

A 0.3-second delay feels like 3.
It's the gap where a thought breaks.

We measure it because we refuse to lose that moment.

What feels "instant" is, in practice, under 0.3s.
That's the line between a captured thought and a forgotten one.

Every design call passes one check first:
"Does this preserve the 0.3 seconds?"
If it doesn't, it doesn't ship — no matter how clever.

Your notes never touch our servers.
We encrypt with AES-GCM on-device and ship them straight to your Gmail.
"We don't store" is the default, not a feature.

Zero ad SDKs. One analytics tag on the marketing site. None in the app itself.
We started by questioning the assumption that convenience requires data.

Ad-supported is the easiest model to add and the hardest to remove.
So we never added it.
Simple to say. Hard to commit to.

Your notes are yours.
So they go to your inbox.
The rest of the philosophy fits in this tweet.

"No one but you can read it" has to be true at the protocol level, not the policy level.
That's why we picked an outbox you keep, not a database we keep.

Adding features is easy.
Refusing to add them is ten times harder.
This isn't really a memo-app problem.

I trust products that can articulate what they removed.
The ones that only list what they added — much less.

There are barely any buttons in this app.
When was the last time you used a UI you could count?

We pick what to delete every week.
Delete day takes 3x more nerve than ship day.

The courage to be "less" is what makes a tool stick.
That's the kind of product we're trying to make.

Wrote half of it in SwiftUI. Threw all of it out. Went back to UIKit.
The reason was 0.3 seconds.
That's the whole story.

Solo dev's strength: no decision gets watered down by committee.
Solo dev's weakness: no one to catch your blind spots.
Both, every day.

One pair of hands defends one user's experience.
That's why this app stays solo.

UIKit isn't fashionable. 0.3 seconds is.
Speed that reaches the user beats novelty that doesn't.

As a solo dev: completely agree.
"Cutting" costs more energy than "building." But not cutting is how products get heavy.
Took me years to learn that the hard way.

One hour a week, just to decide what to delete.
3x harder than deciding what to add. Still on the calendar.

This app does one step well: GTD's Collect.
Organize wherever you already organize.
Each step deserves its own best tool.

In Tiago Forte's CODE, we own the C.
O, D, and E — Notion, Obsidian, wherever you like.

A note that lands in the right moment kills a lot of regret.
Quietly, mid-meeting, 0.3 seconds, into your Gmail.
You won't go back.

When the final destination is Gmail, search becomes Google's job.
No vendor lock. Any OS. Any device.
Your inbox is the world's most underrated personal archive.

The certainty that nothing was lost is what lets you stop organizing.
"Capture is safe → Review can wait."
That order is hard-coded into the product.

The "email-to-self" habit went out of style and is quietly coming back.
It's still the simplest, most durable way to record a thought.

X drafts vanish.
A memo app holds them until you actually send.
Some folks just ship straight to their own inbox and search later.

To everyone who loved Captio:
We rebuilt that feeling on top of modern encryption.

Write, send, forget — in half a second. Again.

In 2024, Captio quietly left the App Store.
Same year, LINE Keep shut down.

A whole category — "just write, just send" in one tap — disappeared overnight.

As someone who used Captio for ten years: that speed was a luxury.
We just wanted that luxury back, with today's encryption and today's offline design.

Note-to-Email is a category that died once in the 2010s.
We're redefining it for 2026.
#NoteToEmail

Your notes' final destination should be your inbox, not an app.
It's where they survive longest, with the least lock-in.
#NoteToEmail

The app you write with and the place you store should be different.
Write on your phone in 0.3s. Store in Gmail forever.
Worth trying that split.
#NoteToEmail

Memo-app speed has been showing up in posts more than it used to.
Six months after LINE Keep, a second wave of users is looking for a new home.

Free for 7 days.
Don't like it? Do nothing. No charge.

iPhone, that's all you need → simplememofast.com/en

Annual = 10 months at the monthly rate.
Two months off, no asterisks.

7-day free trial → simplememofast.com/en

30 seconds to try, with just an iPhone.
If it doesn't fit, just forget it. That's a perfectly fine answer.

simplememofast.com/en

Want to verify the speed claim yourself?
Method and raw data are public. Reproduce away.
→ simplememofast.com/en/blog/fastest-note-app-iphone-2026/
